content.full_text:
F AIR P OLITICAL P RACTICES C OMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 7, 2006

Charles R. Martin
City Manager/City Attorney
City of Temple City
9701 Las Tunas Drive
Temple City, CA 91780-2249

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-05-252

Dear Mr. Martin:

This letter is in response to your request for advice, on behalf of the City of
Temple City ("City"), regarding the mass mail provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").¹ Because the facts presented are of a general nature and do not fully identify the
specific facts involved in the governmental decision at issue, we are treating your request
as a request for informal assistance.²

Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that has already
taken place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political
Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as the finder of fact when it
renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

Please note that there are other bodies of law, separate and apart from the Act's
mass mailing provisions, which may apply to your situation. Laws outside the Act which
may be implicated in the design and publication of the newspaper include, but are not
limited to, sections 1090 et seq. regarding contracts, as well as section 8314 and Penal
Code section 424 which proscribe the use of public moneys for campaign-related

¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. All further references to statutory "sections" will be
to the Government Code and all further references to "regulations" will be to title 2 of the California Code
of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed).

File No. I-05-252
Page 2

activities by state and local governmental entities.³ (See also Stanson v. Mort (1976) 17
Cal.3d 206.)

QUESTION

If the City of Temple makes an advance lump-sum purchase of one full page of
space, each week, for one year in a local (adjudicated) weekly newspaper, would the
mass mailing provisions of the Act apply?

CONCLUSION

Yes. However, if the adjudicated newspaper's items are: (1) published by persons
other than elected officers and (2) are delivered to no more than 200 non-subscribers per
calendar month, the mass mailing prohibitions would not apply. Under regulation
18901(c)(4)(E), only items published by non-elected officers and received by a subscriber
are exempt from the 200-item limit contained in subdivision (a)(4) of regulation 18901.

FACTS⁴

The editor and publisher ("Publisher") of a local adjudicated weekly newspaper⁵
("Newspaper") located in the City has made the following offer to the City: In exchange
for $40,000, the Publisher would provide the City one full page, each week for one year,
for such publicity as the City may deem proper and submit in writing to the Publisher
(i.e., that page will be "owned" by the City to be used as the City sees fit). This would be
a flat charge regardless of whether or not the City is able to fill a page per week. There is
no limit on what the City may place in the provided page, nor whether it includes the
City's legal ads and/or notices.

The Newspaper is a tabloid style publication consisting of approximately 20
pages each issue. If the City does not fill its allotted page, it is unknown whether that

³ The questions you present regarding whether the City of Temple could be "subsidizing" the
weekly newspaper at issue appear to implicate laws outside the purview of the Act, e.g., laws related to the
prohibited gifting of public funds. For comments regarding when (for purposes of a mass mailing analysis
under the Act) an elected officer may be deemed a publisher of a publication, see the subheading in the
Analysis section entitled "Over 200 Mailings Distributed and the 'Unsolicited Request' Exception".

⁴ These facts are based upon those provided in your original correspondence and during a
telephone conversation with staff on March 23, 2006.

⁵ The term "adjudicated newspaper" appears to be synonymous with the phrase "newspaper of
general circulation." (See, e.g., In re Molz (2005) 127 Cal.App.4ᵗʰ 836, 838; see also sections 6000 and
6008 [defining "newspaper of general circulation"].) At least one court has held that there is no
requirement that a publication have a "substantial" number of persons in a particular region to be deemed a
"newspaper of general circulation" (per section 6000), but only that it possess a "bona fide subscription
list" of names – with no maximum limit specified – of persons who are in good faith paying regularly for
their subscriptions. (In re San Diego Commerce (1995) 40 Cal.App.4ᵗʰ 1229, 1232 fn. 5; cf. In re Norwalk
Call (1964) 62 Cal.2d 185, 190 [explaining that "[t]he purpose of limiting publication [of official notices]
to newspapers meeting certain standards is to assure that the published material will come to the attention
of a substantial number of persons in the area affected. . . ."].)

File No. I-05-252
Page 3

page would: (1) remain blank for whatever portion could not be filled; (2) be filled with
whatever materials the City did provide, but would be displayed in a way (perhaps by
increasing the font or line spacing) that would fill the entire page; or (3) be filled with
whatever other materials the Publisher chose to fill it with.

Since the Newspaper is an adjudicated one, it has at least some subscribers.
However, you do not know how many subscribers. You also do not know what
percentage of the Newspaper's annual income or operating budget $40,000 might
represent.

ANALYSIS

A. The Act's Pertinent Mass Mailing Statute And Regulation

In 1988, section 89001 of the Act was added to provide that "[n]o newsletter or
other mass mailing shall be sent at public expense." In order to avoid the absurd result of
literally prohibiting any and all mass mailings created or distributed with public funds,
regardless of their content or purpose, the Commission adopted regulation 18901,
clarifying which mailings were permissible and which were not.

Under regulation 18901, a mass mailing is defined as more than 200 substantially
similar tangible items delivered in a calendar month, by any means, to recipients at their
residence, place of employment, business, or post office box. (See also section 82041.5
and Reg. 18435 [defining "mass mailing"].) The Act's key provision governing mass
mailings is regulation 18901, subdivision (a), which states:

"(a) Except as provided in subdivision (b), a mailing is prohibited by section
89001 if all of the following criteria are met:

"(1) Any item sent is delivered, by any means, to the recipient at his or her
residence, place of employment or business, or post office box. For purposes
of this subdivision (a)(1), the item delivered to the recipient must be a
tangible item, such as a videotape, record, or button, or a written document.

"(2) The item sent either:

"(A) Features an elected officer affiliated with the agency which
produces or sends the mailing, or

"(B) Includes the name, office, photograph, or other reference to an
elected officer affiliated with the agency which produces or sends
the mailing, and is prepared or sent in cooperation, consultation,
coordination, or concert with the elected officer.

File No. I-05-252
Page 4

"(3)    (A) Any of the costs of distribution is paid for with public moneys;
         or

         "(B) Costs of design, production, and printing exceeding $ 50.00 are
         paid with public moneys, and the design, production, or printing is
         done with the intent of sending the item other than as permitted by
         this regulation.

"(4)

         More than two hundred substantially similar items are sent, in a
         single calendar month, excluding any item sent in response to an
         unsolicited request and any item described in subdivision (b)."

In order for an item to be a prohibited "mass mailing" as defined under the Act,
all four, enumerated criteria listed above must be met. (See Reg. 18901, suds.(a)(1) –
(a)(4).) If all four criteria under subdivision (a) apply, the mass mailing shall be
prohibited, barring the application of any exceptions. (See e.g., Reg. 18901, suds.(b)(1)
– (b)(11) [listing the general exceptions to the Act's mass mailing prohibition].)

B. Transmission Of A "Tangible" Item

    Subdivision (a)(1) of regulation 18901, only restricts items that are mailed or
delivered, by any means, to a person's home, office or post office box. If items are set
out for the public to pick up on their own, or are handed out in a public area, the
restrictions of the regulation do not apply to those items. Copies of a publication picked
up at City offices or passed out at meetings also do not count toward the 200-item limit.
(Reg. 18901(c)(4)(A); Moore Advice Letter, No. A-93-307; Waggoner Advice Letter,
No. A-92-247; Alguist Advice Letter, No. I-89-109.) You do not know how many
subscribers receive the Newspaper from either a newspaper carrier or through the mail.
Therefore, the criterion in subdivision (a)(1) may or may not be met.

C. "Elected Officer"

    The second of the four criteria under regulation 18901, subdivision (a), indicates
that if reference is made to an "elected officer," the contemplated mailing could be
prohibited. (Reg. 18901(a)(2).)

    According to the Act, an "elected officer" is any person who holds an elective
office, has been elected to an elective office but has not yet taken office, or who is
appointed to fill a vacant elective office. (Section 82020.) "Elective office" means any
state, regional, county, municipal, district, or judicial office which is filled at an election,
as well as membership on a county central committee of a qualified political party, and
members elected to the Board of Administration of the Public Employees' Retirement
System (PERS). (Section 82023.)

File No. I-05-252
Page 5

D. References To Elected Officers Affiliated With The Agency

Subdivision (a)(2) of regulation 18901, generally describes two categories of
mass mailings which are regulated under the Act: (A) items that feature an elected officer
affiliated with the agency producing or sending the mailing, and (B) items that do not
necessarily feature, but include, a reference to an elected officer affiliated with the
agency and are prepared or sent in cooperation with that elected officer.⁶ You should be
aware that these two general restrictions have some exceptions found in subdivision (b)
of regulation 18901.

Regardless, a mass mailing which does not include any reference to an elected
officer affiliated with the agency that produces or distributes the mailing is not subject to
the restrictions of the regulation. (Reg. 18901, subd.(a)(2); Dale Advice Letter, No. A-02-
331.) An "[e]lected officer affiliated with the agency" is defined as an elected officer
who is a "member, officer, or employee of the agency, or of a subunit thereof such as a
committee, or who has supervisory control over the agency, or who appoints one or more
members of the agency." (Reg. 18901, subd.(c)(1).)

1. Items Featuring An Elected Officer

If a mass mailing "features" an elected officer, it is subject to regulation 18901.
(See Reg. 18901, subd.(a)(2)(A).) A mass mailing "[f]eatures an elected officer" if it
includes the elected officer's photograph or signature, or singles out the elected officer by
the manner of display of his or her name or office in the layout of the document, such as
by headlines, captions, type size, typeface, or type color. (Reg. 18901, subd.(c)(2); see
also Reg. 18901, subd. (b)(9)(B).)

Therefore, any tangible mass mailing item including any photographs of an
elected officer affiliated with the agency is prohibited. (Reg. 18901, suds.(a)(2)(A),
(b)(9)(B) and (c)(2).)

2. Items Referencing An Elected Officer, The Preparation Or
Sending Of Which Is Accomplished In Cooperation With The Official

If a mass mailing merely includes a reference to, but does not necessarily feature,
an elected officer affiliated with the agency which produces or sends the mailing, and is
prepared or sent in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with the elected
officer, it is subject to regulation 18901. (Reg. 18901, subd.(a)(2)(B).) A mass mailing
references an elected official when it includes the name, office, photograph, or other
reference to that person. (Ibid.)

⁶ The "prepared and sent in cooperation with that elected officer" portion of subsection (B) is
already met in the case you describe since the elected officials of the City would vote to purchase space in
the Newspaper.

File No. 1-05-252
Page 6

E. Public Money

Pursuant to subdivision (a)(3), the "public money" criterion of the regulation is
met if: "(A) Any of the costs of distribution are paid for with public moneys; or (B) Costs of design, production, and printing exceeding $ 50.00 are paid with public moneys,
and the design, production, or printing is done with the intent of sending the item other
than as permitted by this regulation."

Even if a newspaper is only partially funded with public money, the mailing must
comply with section 89001. (Moore Advice Letter, No. A-99-234; cf. Curtis Advice
Letter, No. A-94-053.) This means that even if part of the publication of the newspaper
is funded through money received from those purchasing advertising space, the "public
money" criterion is met if any of the costs of the newspaper's distribution is paid for with
public funds, or if over $50 of design, production, or printing costs are paid for with
public funds. (See Houston Advice Letter, No. I-05-183; Schectman Advice Letter, No.
I-03-233.)

With specific regard to advertisements in publicly-funded publications, the
Houston Advice Letter, No. A-04-026, states that the prohibitions against mass mailings
at public expense would apply to an advertisement placed and paid for by a city council
member for his accounting firm in a chamber of commerce newsletter that was funded in
part by city funds. On the other hand, such prohibitions do not apply to political
advertisements placed in a newsletter put out by a fire protection district, if no tax funds
or tax-supported personnel are used in the production or distribution of the newsletter.
(Smith Advice Letter, No. A-98-117.)

Since $40,000 per year of gross revenue to the Newspaper would come from
"public money," due to the contemplated contract between the Newspaper and the City,
this element would be met.

F. Over 200 Mailings Distributed And The "Unsolicited Request" Exception

Pursuant to subdivision (a)(4) of regulation 18901, a mass mailing is subject to
regulation if "[m]ore than two hundred substantially similar items are sent, in a single
calendar month, excluding any item sent in response to an unsolicited request" and does
not fit an exception listed in subdivision (b). (See definitions of, e.g., "substantially
similar" [in subdivision (c)(3)] and "unsolicited request" [in subdivision (c)(4)]; see also
the subdivision (b) exceptions, described below.)⁷

An "unsolicited request" for a mass mailing, which does not count toward the
200-mailings limit, is defined in the following five different categories contained in
regulation 18901(c)(4): (A) communications, such as petitions, requesting a response to
someone other than an elected officer or that officer's agent; (B) a request for continuing
information for a period not longer than two years; (C) a mass mailing recipient's

⁷ See also analysis under "Transmission Of A 'Tangible' Item", supra.

File No. 1-05-252
Page 7

response to certain agency notices which indicate that, in the absence of a response, the
recipient's name will be purged from the mailing list for that mass mailing; (D) a
communication sent in re response to an elected officer's participation at a public forum,
press conference, or issuance of a press release; and, (E) a request for a subscription to a
newspaper or other periodical published by non-elected officers. (Reg. 18901(c)(4)(A) –
(E).)

The last exception described above (Reg. 18901(c)(4)(E))⁸ appears to be
particularly pertinent to the case you describe since the publication at issue is an
"adjudicated" newspaper and is not published by elected officers. Pursuant to that
provision, we have advised that a notice of a public hearing published in a newspaper of
general circulation and delivered to subscribers is not a mass mailing. Ujenkins
Advice Letter, No. A-01-213; see Valenti Advice Letter, No. I-91-264 [prohibiting use of
signature of elected city clerk in notices of public hearings delivered to more than 200
residents in a calendar month unless mailing is legally required and inclusion of clerk's
signature is necessary to mailing].) In fact, the Commission has advised several times in
the past that various kinds of government notifications published in subscriber
publications are not subject to the mass mailing prohibitions in the Act.

In the Warren Advice Letter, No. A-93-152, a newspaper of general circulation
could publish and distribute, at public expense, a "tabloid insert" whose content was
created by the Modesto Irrigation District because it would be deemed to be an
"unsolicited request." It is assumed that the newspaper would do the layout work based
upon the specifications given to it by the District. The information (regarding the
District's operations, educational services, energy management programs and elected
officials) would include the name, photograph, and area of representation of each
director.

In the Sluder Advice letter, No. A-93-089, a newspaper of general circulation (not
published by the City of Barstow) could publish, at public expense, "a promotional
letter . . . to be signed by the mayor" of the city as an advertisement or letter because it
would be deemed to be an "unsolicited request." The letter would contain the mayor's
signature, but would not refer to a recall election against him.

In the Lipton Advice Letter, No. A-92-476, a newspaper of general circulation
(not published by the hospital district at issue there) could publish, at public expense,
Marin Hospital District's annual report as an advertisement or as an insert because it
would be deemed to be an "unsolicited request."

In the Weckerle Advice Letter, No. A-92-464, a newspaper of general circulation
could publish, at public expense, an advertisement which would solicit members of the

⁸ Reg. 18901(c)(4)(E) states: "A person who subscribes to newspapers or other periodicals
published by persons other than elected officers shall be deemed to have made unsolicited requests for
materials published in those subscription publications."

⁹ See footnote 5, supra.

File No. 1-05-252
Page 8

public to apply for a position on the City of Hemet's new economic development
commission, since it was deemed to be an "unsolicited request." Like the Valenti Advice
Letter, supra, this letter broadly pronounced that: "the mass mailing restrictions do not
apply to advertisements placed in newspapers of general circulation which are delivered
to subscribers."

In the Geiogue Advice Letter, No. A-91-193, a general circulation newspaper
could publish an advertisement for the sale of college savings bonds paid for by the
state treasurer since it was deemed an "unsolicited request." This letter also broadly
concludes that "[t]he mass mailing provisions of the Act do not apply to paid
advertisements in newspapers of general circulation which are distributed to subscribers."

A Note On Whether A Publication Is "Published By Persons Other Than Elected
Officers." All of the advice letters interpreting regulation 18901(c)(4)(E) and cited above
implicitly presume that the newspaper of general circulation at issue was "published by
persons other than elected officers."¹⁰

In conclusion, any item published by a non-elected officer and received by a
subscriber (or received at some location other than the a non-subscribing recipient's
residence, place of employment or business, or post office box)¹¹ will not count towards
the 200-item per month limit in subdivision (a)(4). In other words, if (for example) a
publication was not published by an elected officer and was distributed only to
subscribers and people who picked them up at the City's offices, the mass mailing
prohibitions of the Act would not apply. On the other hand, if a newspaper published by
a non-elected officer had 100 subscribers, but delivered over 200 substantially similar
items per month to 500 recipients at their residence, place of employment or business, or
post office box, then the mass mailing prohibitions of the Act would apply.

___________________________
¹⁰ Whether a vote by a local government's elected officers to make a payment of public funds to a
local publication would make such elected officers "publishers," in the context of regulation
18901(c)(4)(E), has never been analyzed. But the type of information the Commission would need to
analyze such a question here has not been provided (e.g., What does it cost to publish the Newspaper?
What is the fair market value of a one-page ad in the Newspaper? Does the publisher give volume
discounts on ad purchases? If so, has such a discount ever been given to others in the past? If so, describe.
Would such a purchase affect the Newspaper's coverage of local governmental affairs?) Therefore, we
cannot determine whether, in this instance, a vote by elected officers to go forward with the $40,000
transaction described would make such elected officers de facto "publishers" of the Newspaper in the
context of subdivision (c)(4)(F).

¹¹ See 18901(a)(1) and footnote 7, supra.

File No. 1-05-252
Page 9

G. A Note About The Subdivision (b) Exceptions

Regulation 18901 provides a variety of additional exceptions to the four-criteria
prohibition provided above. These exceptions are contained in subdivision (b) of the
regulation. For instance, under subdivisions (b)(3) and (b)(4), regulation 18901 exempts
inter-governmental and intra-governmental mailings sent in the normal course of
business. Therefore, mailings which meet the criteria in subdivision (a) and which are
sent in the normal course of business to other governmental entities or officers, or to
intra-agency employees and other staff, will not be prohibited. (Reg. 18901(b)(3),
(b)(4).)

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Andreas C. Rockas
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosures

sections.analysis:
A. The Act's Pertinent Mass Mailing Statute And Regulation
In 1988, section 89001 of the Act was added to provide that "[n]o newsletter or
other mass mailing shall be sent at public expense." In order to avoid the absurd result of
literally prohibiting any and all mass mailings created or distributed with public funds,
regardless of their content or pu{pose, the Commission adopted regulation 18901,
clarifying which mailings were pernissible and which were not.
Under regulation 1 8901 , a mass mailing is defined as more than 2OO substantially
similar tangible items delivered in a calendar month, by any means, to recipients at their
residence, place of employment, business, or post office box. (See also section B2O4l.5
and Reg. 18435 [defining "mass mailing"].) The Act's keyprovision governing mass
mailings is regulation 18901, subdivision (a), which states:
"(a) Except as provided in subdivision (b), a mailing is prohibited by section
89001 if all of the following criteria are met:
"(1) Any item sent is delivered,by any means, to the recipient at his or her
residence, place of employment or business, or post office box. For purposes
of this subdivision (aXl), the item delivered to the recipient must be a
tangible item, such as a videotape, record, or button, or a written document.
"(2)The item sent either:
"(A) Features an elected officer affiliated with the agency which
produces or sends the mailing, or
"(B) Includes the name, office, photograph, or other reference to an
elected officer affiliated with the agency which produces or sends
the mailing, and is prepared or sent in cooperation, consultation,
coordination, or concert with the elected officer.
"(3) (A) AnV of the costs of distribution is paid for with public moneys;
or
"(B) Costs of design, production, and printing exceeding $ 50.00 are
paid with public moneys, and the design, production, or printing is
done with the intent of sending the item other than as permitted by
this regulation.
"(4)
More than two hundred substantially similar items are sent, in a
single calendar month, excluding any item sent in response to an
unsolicited request and any item described in subdivision (b)."
In order for an item to be a prohibited "mass mailing" as defined under the Act,
all four, enumerated criteria listed above must be met. (See Reg. 18901, subds.(a)(l) -
(aX4).) If all four criteria under subdivision (a) apply, the mass mailing shall be
prohibited, barring the application of any exceptions. (See e.g., Reg. 1g901, subds.(b)(l)
- (bX11) [listing the general exceptions to the Act's mass mailing prohibition].)
B. Transmission OfA .oTangible, Item
Subdivision (a)(1) of regulation 18901, only restricts items that are mailed or
delivered, by any means, to a person's home, office or post office box. If items are set
out for the public to pick up on their own, or are handed out in a public area, the
restrictions of the regulation do not apply to those items. Copies of a publication picked
up at City offices or passed out at meetings also do not count toward the 200-item limit.
(Reg. 18901(c)(a)(A); Moore Advice Letter, No. 4-93-307; waggoner Adviceletter,
No. 4-92-247; Alquist Advice Letter, No. I-89-109.) you do not know how many
subscribers receive the Newspaper from either a newspaper carrier or through the mail.
Therefore, the criterion in subdivision (a)(1) may or may not be met.
C. 6'Elected Officer"
The second of the four criteria under regulation 18901, subdivision (a), indicates
that if reference is made to an "elected officer," the contemplated mailing could be
prohibited. (Reg. 18901 (a)(z).)
According to the Act, an "elected officer" is any person who holds an elective
ffice, has been elected to an elective ffice but has not yet taken office, or who is
appointed to fill avacant elective ffice. (Section 82020.) "Elective office" means any
state, regional, county, municipal, district, or judicial office which is filled at art election,
as well as membership on a county central committee of a qualified political party, and
members elected to the Board of Administration of the Public Employees' Retirement
System (PERS). (Section 82023.)
D. References To Elected officers Affiliated with rhe Agency
Subdivision (a)(2) of regulation 18901, generally describes two categories of
mass mailings which areregiated under the Act: (A) items that feature an elected officer
affiliated with the agency producing or sending the mailing, and (B) items that do not
necessarily feature, but include, a reference to an elected officer affiliated with the
agency and are prepared or sent in cooperation with that elected officer.6 You should be
aware that these two general restrictions have some exceptions found in subdivision (b)
ofregulation 18901.
Regardless, a mass mailing which does not include any reference to an elected
officer affiliated with the agency that produces or distributes the mailing is not subject to
the restrictions of the regulation. (Reg. 18901, subd.(a)(2); Dale Advice Letter,No. A-02-
331.) An "[e]lected officer affiliated with the agency" is defined as an elected officer
who is a "member, officer, or anployee of the agency, or of a subunit thereof such as a
committee, or who has supervisory control over the agency, or who appoints one or more
members of the agency." (Reg. 18901, subd.(c)(l).)
1. Items Featuring An Elected Officer
If a mass mailing "features" an elected officer, it is subject to regulation 18901.
(See Reg. 18901, subd.(a)(2)(A).) A mass mailing "[fleatures an elected officer,,if it
includes the elected officer's photograph or signature, or singles out the elected officer by
the manner of display of his or her name or office in the layout of the document, such as
by headlines, captions, type size, typeface, or type color. (Reg. 1g901, subd.(c)(2); see
also Reg. 18901, subd. (bX9XB).)
Therefore, any tangible mass mailing item including any photographs of an
elected officer affiliated with the agency is prohibited. (Reg. 1g901, suuas.la;12;1e;,
(b)(eXB) and (c)(2).)
2. Items Referencing An Elected officer, The preparation or
Sending Of Which Is Aecomplished In Cooperation With The Official
If a mass mailing merely includes a reference to, but does not necessarily feature,
an elected officer affiliated with the agency which produces or sends the mailing, and is
prepared or sent in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with the elected
officer, it is subject to regulation 18901. (Reg. 18901, subd.(a)(2)(B).) A mass mailing
references an elected official when it includes the name, office, photograph, or other
reference to that person. (Ibid.)
u The "prepared and sent in cooperation with that elected officer" portion of subsection (B) is
akeady met in the case you describe since the elected officials of the City would vote to purchase space in
the Newspaper.
E. Public Money
Pursuant to subdivision (a)(3), the "public money" criterion ofthe regulation is
met if: "(A) Any of the costs of distribution are paid for with public -orr"yr; or tlll 13)
Costs of design, production, and printing exceeding $ 50.00 are paid with public -o.,"yr,
and the design, production, or printing is done with the intent of sending the item other
than as permitted by this regulation."
Even if a newspaper is only partially funded with public money, the mailing must
comply with section 89001 . (Moore Advice Letter, No. 4-99-234; cf. Curtis Adviie
Letter, No. 4-94-053.) This means that even ifpart of the publication of the newspaper
is funded through money received from those purchasing advertising space, the . publlc
money" criterion is met if any of the costs of the newspaper's distribution is paid for with
public funds, or if over $50 of design, production, or printing costs are paid for with
public funds. (See Houston Advice Letter, No. I-05-183; Schectman Adviceletter, No.
I-03-233.)
With specific regard to advertisements in publicly-funded publications, the
Houston Advice Letter, No. 4-04-026, states that the prohibitions against mass mailings
at public expense would apply to an advertisement placed and paid for by a city council
member for his accounting firm in a chamber of commerce newsletter that was funded in
partby city funds. On the other hand, such prohibitions do not apply to political
advertisements placed in a newsletter put out by a fire protection district, if no tax funds
or tax-supported personnel are used in the production or distribution of the newsletter.
(Smith Advice Letter, No. A-98-117.)
Since $40,000 per year of gross revenue to the Newspaper would come from
'public money," due to the contemplated contract between the Newspaper and the City,
this element would be met.
F. Over 200 Mailings Distributed And The o6Unsolicited Request,, Exception
Pursuant to subdivision (a)(4) of regulation 18901, a mass mailing is subject to
regulation if "[m]ore than two hundred substantially similar iterns u.e rrrr1, in a single
calendar month, excluding any item sent in response to an unsolicited request,, and does
not fit an exception listed in subdivision (b). (See definitions of, e.g., "substantially
similar" [in subdivision (c)(3)] and'lrnsolicited r5:quest" [in subdivision (c)(a)]; see also
the subdivision (b) exceptions, described below.)7
An'lrnsolicited request" for a mass mailing, which does not count toward the
2}}-mailings limit, is defined in the following five different categories contained in
regulation 18901(c)(a): (A) communications, such as petitions, requesting a response to
someone other than an elected officer or that officer's agent; (B) a requesi for continuing
information for a period not longer than two years; (C) a mass mailing recipient,s
7 See also analysis under "Transmission Of A ,Tangible, Item,,, supra
response to certain agency notices which indicate that, in the absence of a response, the
recipient's name will be purged from the mailing list for that mass mailing; (D) a
communication sent in response to an elected officer's participation at apublic forum,
press conference, or issuance ofa press release; and, (E) a request for a subscription to a
newspaper or other periodical published by non-elected officers. (Reg. 1S901(c)(aXA) -
(E).)
The last exception described above (Reg. 18901 (c)(a)(E))8 appears to be
particularly pertinent to the case you describe since the publication at issue is an
"adjudicated" newspaper and is not published by elected officers. Pursuant to that
provision, we have advised that anotice of a public hearing published in a newspaper of
general circulatione that is delivered to subscribers is not amass mailing. (Jenkins
Advice Letter, No. A-01-213; see Yalenti Advice Letter, No. I-91-264 [prohibiting use of
signature of elected city clerk in notices of public hearings delivered to more than20O
residents in a calendar month unless mailing is legally required and inclusion of clerk,s
signature is necessary to mailing].) In fact, the Commission has advised several times in
the past that various kinds of government notifications published in subscriber
publications are not subject to the mass mailing prohibitions in the Act.
In the Warren Advice Letter, No. A-93-152, a newspaper of general circulation
could publish and distribute, at public expense, a 66tabloid insert" whose content was
created by the Modesto Irrigation District because it would be deemed to be an
"unsolicited request." It is assumed that the newspaper would do the layout work based
upon the specifications given to it by the District. The information (regarding the
District's operations, educational services, energy management programs and elected
officials) would include the name, photograph, and areaof representation of each
director.
In the Sluder Advice letter, No. 4-93-089, a newspaper of general circulation (not
published by the City of Barstow) could publish, at public expense, "a promotional
Ietter . . . to be signed by the mayor" of the city as an advertisement or letter because it
would be deemed to be an 'lrnsolicited request." The letter would contain the mayor,s
signature, but would not refer to a recall election against him.
In the Lipton Advice Letter, No. 4-92-476, a newspaper of general circulation
(not published by the hospital district at issue there) could publish, at public expense,
Marin llospital District's annual report as an advertisement or as an insert because it
would be deemed to be an "unsolicited request."
In the Weckerle Advice Letter, No. 4-92-464, a newspaper of general circulation
could publish, at public expense, an advertisement which would solicit members of the
t R.g. 18901(c)(a)(E) states: "A person who subscribes to newspapers or other periodicals
published by persons other than elected officers shall be deemed to have made unsolicited requests for
materials published in those subscription publications.,,
e See footnote 5, supra.
public to apply for a position on the City of Hemetos new economic development
commission, since it was deemed to be an'lrnsolicited request." Like the Valenti Advice
Letter, supra, this letter broadly pronounced that: "the mass mailing restrictions do not
ryply to advertisements placed in newspapers of general circulation which are delivered
to subscribers."
In the Geiogue Advice Letter, No. 4-91-193, a general circulation newspaper
could publish an advertisement for the sale of college savings bonds paid for by the
state treasurer since it was deemed an'tnsolicited request." This letter also broadly
concludes that "[t]he mass mailing provisions of the Act do not apply to paid
advertisements in newspapers of general circulation which are distributed to subscribers."
A Note On Wether A Publication Is "Published By Persons Other Than Elected
Officers. " All of the advice letters interpreting regulation 18901(c)(a)@) and cited above
implicitly presume that the newspaper of general circulation at issue was "published by
persons other than elected officers."l0
In conclusion, any itern published by a non-elected officer and received by a
subscriber (or received at some location other than the a non-subscribing recipient's
residence, place of employment or business, or post office box)l I will not count towards
the 200-item per month limit in subdivision (aXa). In other words, if (for example) a
publication was not published by a., elected officer and was distributed only to
subscribers and people who picked them up at the City's offices, the mass mailing
prohibitions of the Act would not apply. On the other hand, if a newspaper published by
a non-elected officer had 100 subscribers, but delivered over 200 substantially similar
items per month to 500 recipients at their residence, place of employment or business, or
post office box, then the mass mailing prohibitions of the Act would apply.
r0 Whether a vote by a local government's elected officers to make a payment of public funds to a
local publication would make such elected officers "publishers," in the context of regulation
18901(c)(a)@), has never been analyzed. But the type of information the Commission would need to
anLalyze such a question here has not been provided (e.g., What does it cost to publish the Newspaper?
What is the fair market value of a one-page ad in the Newspaper? Does the publisher give volume
discounts on ad purchases? Ifso, has such a discount ever been given to others in the past? If so, describe.
Would such a purchase affect the Newspaper's coverage of local governmental affairs?) Therefore, we
cannot determine whether, in this instance, a vote by elected officers to go forward with the $40,000
transaction described would make such elected officers de facto "publishers" of the Newspaper in the
context of subdivision (c)(a)@).
il See 18901(a)(1) and footnote 7, supra.
G. A Note About The Subdivision (b) Exceptions
Regulation 18901 provides avaiety of additional exceptions to the four-criteria
prohibition described above. Those exceptions are contained in subdivision (b) of the
regulation. For instance, under subdivisions (b)(3) and (b)(a), regulation 13901 exempts
inter-governmental and intra-governmental mailings sent in the normal course of
business. Therefore, mailings which meet the criteria in subdivision (a) and which are
sent in the normal course of business to other govemmental entities or officers, or to
intra-agency employees and other staff, will not be prohibited. (Reg. l g90l (bX3),
(bx4).)

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: If the City of Temple makes an advance lump-sum purchase of one full page of
space, each week, for one year in a local (adjudicated) weekly newspaper, would the
mass mailing provisions of the Act apply?

CONCLUSION: Yes. However, if the adjudicated newspaper's items are: (1) published by persons
other than elected officers and (2) are delivered to no more than 200 non-subscriteis per
calendar month, the mass mailing prohibitions would not apply. Under regulation
18901(c)(a)@), only items published by non-elected officers and receivedly a subscriber
are exempt from the 200-item limit contained in subdivision (a)(4) of regulaiion 1g901.
FACTS4
The editor and publisher ("Publisher") of a local adjudicated weekly newspapers
("Newspaper") located in the City has made the following offer to the City: tn "*"h*g"
for $40,000, the Publisher would provide the City one full pa1e, each week for one year,
for such publicity as the City may deem proper and submit in writing to the publisher
(i.e', that page will be "owned" by the City to be used as the City sees fit). This would be
a flat charge regardless of whether or not the City is able to fill apageper week. There is
no limit on what the City may place in the provided page, nor whetherlt includes the
City's legal ads atd/or notices.
The Newspaper is a tabloid style publication consisting of approximately 20
pages each issue. If the City does not fill its allotted page, it is unknown whether that
3 The questions you present regarding whether the City of Temple could be ,.subsidizing,, the
weekly newspaper at issue appear to implicate laws outside the purview of th" A.t, e.g., laws related to the
prohibited gifting ofpublic funds. For comments regarding when (forpurposes ofa riass mailing analysis
under the Act) an elected officer may be deemed a publisher of a publication, see the subheadingln tha
Analysis section entitled "Over 200 Mailings Dishibuted and the iUnsolicited Request, Exception,,.
a These facts are based upon those provided in your original correspondence and during a
telephone conversation with staff on March 23,2006.
5 The term "adjudicated newspaper" appears to be synorlymous with the phrase ,,newspaper of
general circulation." (See, e.g., In re Molz (2005) 127 Cal.App.4m 836, 838; ,"" ,Lo sections 6000 and
6008 [defining "newspaper of general circulation"].) At teasi one court has held that there is no
requirement that a publication have a "substantial" number of persons in a particular region to be deemed a
"newspaper ofgeneral circulation" (per section 6000), but only that it possess a "bona fide subscription
list" of names - with no minimum limit specified - of persons who ary in good faith paying regulaily for
their subscriptions. (In re San Diego Commerce (1995) 40 Cal.App4'h 1219,1232 fi.^. s; i. ti re Norwalk
Call (1964) 62 Cal.2d' 185, 190 [explaining that "[t]he purpose of limiting publication Jototiciat noticesl
to newspapers meeting certain standards is to assure that the published material will come to the attention
of a substantial number ofpersons in the area affected. . . .,'].)
page would: ( 1 ) remain blank for whatever portion could not be filled; (2) be filled with
whatever materials the City did provide, but would be displayed in a way (perhaps by
increasing the font or line spacing) that would fill the entire page; or (3) be filled with
whatever other materials the Publisher chose to fill it with.
Since the Newspaper is an adjudicated one, it has at least some subscribers.
However, you do not know how many subscribers. you also do not know what
percentage of the Newspaper's annual income or operating budget $40,000 might
represent.
embedding.first_500_words: F AIR P OLITICAL P RACTICES C OMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 July 7, 2006 Charles R. Martin City Manager/City Attorney City of Temple City 9701 Las Tunas Drive Temple City, CA 91780-2249 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-05-252 Dear Mr. Martin: This letter is in response to your request for advice, on behalf of the City of Temple City ("City"), regarding the mass mail provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because the facts presented are of a general nature and do not fully identify the specific facts involved in the governmental decision at issue, we are treating your request as a request for informal assistance.² Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that has already taken place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as the finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Please note that there are other bodies of law, separate and apart from the Act's mass mailing provisions, which may apply to your situation. Laws outside the Act which may be implicated in the design and publication of the newspaper include, but are not limited to, sections 1090 et seq. regarding contracts, as well as section 8314 and Penal Code section 424 which proscribe the use of public moneys for campaign-related ¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. All further references to statutory "sections" will be to the Government Code and all further references to "regulations" will be to title 2 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. ² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed). File No. I-05-252 Page 2 activities by state and local governmental entities.³ (See also Stanson v. Mort (1976) 17 Cal.3d 206.) QUESTION If the City of Temple makes an advance lump-sum purchase of one full page of space, each week, for one year in a local (adjudicated) weekly newspaper, would the mass mailing provisions of the Act apply? CONCLUSION Yes. However, if the adjudicated newspaper's items are: (1) published by persons other than elected officers and (2) are delivered to no more than 200 non-subscribers per calendar month, the mass mailing prohibitions would not apply. Under regulation 18901(c)(4)(E), only items published by non-elected officers and received by a subscriber are exempt from the 200-item limit contained in subdivision (a)(4) of regulation 18901. FACTS⁴ The editor and publisher ("Publisher") of a local adjudicated weekly newspaper⁵ ("Newspaper") located in the City has made the following offer to the City: In exchange for $40,000, the Publisher would provide the City one full page, each week for one year, for such publicity as the City may deem proper
